spec: Evaluate the cat's proportional occupation of the frame. Determine how much of the image the cat physically fills, considering not just raw spatial coverage but whether the cat feels wholly and comfortably present within the frame. A cat that occupies a generous portion of the image commands more visual weight, but a close-up that clips the cat's body and leaves the subject feeling incomplete should not be rewarded the same as one where the cat is substantially and deliberately contained. Score higher (closer to 1) when the cat's scale relative to the frame feels generous and authoritative — the cat is large, fully visible, and takes up a substantial share of the image. Score lower (closer to 0) when the cat is small, distant, takes up only a tiny fraction of the available space, or is cropped in a way that makes the subject feel incomplete rather than commanding.

Evaluate the cat's positional prominence within the frame. Determine where the cat is placed relative to the center of the image and the natural focal lines where the human eye tends to rest, such as the rule-of-thirds intersections. A cat positioned in these zones of visual gravity benefits from the way human perception works — the eye arrives at it immediately and without effort. Score higher (closer to 1) when the cat is centrally placed or sits along prominent compositional lines, occupying a position of natural visual gravity where the viewer's eye falls first. Score lower (closer to 0) when the cat is pushed to the far edges, tucked into corners, or positioned in peripheral areas of the frame where the viewer must actively search to find it. Positional prominence is about compositional placement, not size — even a modestly sized cat can score well if it occupies the frame's natural focal point.

Evaluate the scene clarity surrounding the cat. Determine the degree to which the background and surrounding environment yield to the cat, allowing it to stand as the unambiguous subject of the image. Consider whether the space around the cat is clean and simple — letting the cat visually stand out without competition — or whether it is cluttered, chaotic, and filled with competing visual elements that pull the viewer's attention away. Also consider whether objects partially obstruct the cat, whether patterns or colors camouflage it, or whether the cat blends into its surroundings in a way that makes it visually indistinct. Score higher (closer to 1) when the scene cooperates with the cat's dominance — the background is clean, uncluttered, and the cat is visually distinct from its surroundings. Score lower (closer to 0) when visual noise, clutter, competing objects, obstructions, or camouflage undermine the cat's authority as the clear, unambiguous subject of the photograph.
